subject: Properties of Expectation
topic: §47 Covariance, Variance of Sums, and Correlations
question: Given: $E(X) = 10$, $\mathrm{Var}(X) = 25$, $E(Y) = 50$, $\mathrm{Var}(Y) = 100$, $E(Z) = 6$, $\mathrm{Var}(Z) = 4$, $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y) = 10$, and $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Z) = 3.5$. Let $Z = X + cY$. Find $c$ if $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Z) = 3.5$.
explanation: We have $$\mathrm{Cov}(X,Z) = \mathrm{Cov}(X, X+cY) = \mathrm{Cov}(X,X) + c\,\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathrm{Var}(X) + c\,\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y) = 25 + c(10) = 3.5$$ Solving for $c$ we find $c = -2.15$. Using (b) and (d) in the previous theorem with $Y_j = X_j$, $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ we find $$\mathrm{Var}\left( \sum_{i=1}^n X_i \right) = \mathrm{Cov}\left( \sum_{i=1}^n X_i, \sum_{i=1}^n X_i \right) = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n \mathrm{Cov}(X_i,X_j) = \sum_{i=1}^n \mathrm{Var}(X_i) + \sum_{i\ne j}\sum \mathrm{Cov}(X_i,X_j)$$ Since each pair of indices $i\ne j$ appears twice in the double summation, the above reduces to $$\mathrm{Var}\left( \sum_{i=1}^n X_i \right) = \sum_{i=1}^n \mathrm{Var}(X_i) + 2\sum_{i<j}\sum \mathrm{Cov}(X_i,X_j).$$ In particular, if $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ are pairwise independent then $\mathrm{Var}\left( \sum_{i=1}^n X_i \right) = \sum_{i=1}^n \mathrm{Var}(X_i)$.
